question_text: The law of demand states that, other things remaining constant:
explanation: The law of demand states that there is an inverse (negative) relationship between the price of a good and the quantity demanded — as price rises, quantity demanded falls, and vice versa, ceteris paribus (other things remaining constant). This 'other things' includes income, prices of related goods, tastes, expectations, and number of buyers. The law is reflected in the downward-sloping demand curve and is one of the most fundamental laws in economics.
subject: economics

options.text: As price increases, quantity demanded decreases
As price increases, quantity demanded also increases
As income increases, quantity demanded decreases
As price increases, supply decreases

elimination_hints.hint: A positive price-quantity relationship would describe a Giffen good or Veblen good — exceptions to the law, not the law itself. The law of demand specifically describes an inverse relationship.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the general law (inverse relationship) with exceptions like Giffen goods.

elimination_hints.hint: The relationship between income and demand is described by income effect and Engel curves, not the law of demand. The law of demand specifically relates PRICE to quantity demanded.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the income-demand relationship with the price-demand relationship.

elimination_hints.hint: Supply is a producer-side concept. The law of demand is about consumer behaviour — how quantity DEMANDED changes with price. Supply has its own separate law.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up demand (consumer) with supply (producer) concepts.

question_text: The demand curve slopes downward from left to right because:
explanation: The demand curve slopes downward primarily because of the law of diminishing marginal utility. As a consumer buys more units, the additional satisfaction (MU) from each successive unit falls. Consumers are willing to pay less for additional units since they derive less satisfaction from them. Other reasons include the income effect (price fall increases real income), substitution effect (cheaper good is substituted for costlier alternatives), and new buyers entering the market at lower prices.
subject: economics

options.text: Higher prices attract more buyers
As price falls, consumers buy more due to income effect, substitution effect, and diminishing marginal utility
Producers reduce supply at higher prices
Government imposes price controls

elimination_hints.hint: Higher prices repel buyers, not attract them (for normal goods). The demand curve slopes downward precisely because fewer consumers are willing to buy at higher prices.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the price-demand relationship — thinking higher prices attract more buyers.

elimination_hints.hint: Supply behaviour is irrelevant to the demand curve's slope. The demand curve reflects consumer behaviour only. Producers' supply decisions are shown on the supply curve.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up demand curve (consumer) with supply curve (producer) explanations.

elimination_hints.hint: Government price controls are external interventions, not the reason demand curves slope downward. The downward slope exists naturally due to consumer psychology (diminishing MU) and budget effects.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing the natural shape of the demand curve to government intervention.

question_text: Which of the following is an exception to the law of demand?
explanation: Giffen goods are the classic exception to the law of demand. For a Giffen good, as price rises, quantity demanded also INCREASES — the demand curve slopes upward. This paradox occurs because for very inferior goods that form a large share of a poor consumer's budget (like coarse grain for a poor family), the income effect of a price rise (reduced real income forces more consumption of the cheap staple) outweighs the substitution effect (switching to alternatives). This was observed by Sir Robert Giffen in 19th-century Ireland with bread consumption.
subject: economics

options.text: Normal goods
Complementary goods
Giffen goods
Substitute goods

elimination_hints.hint: Normal goods follow the law of demand perfectly — as price rises, demand falls. They are called 'normal' precisely because they behave as the law predicts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking normal goods are exceptions when they are the standard case the law describes.

elimination_hints.hint: Complementary goods (tea and sugar, car and petrol) follow the law of demand for each individual good. Cross-price relationships between complements don't make them exceptions to the law.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the concept of complementary goods with exceptions to the law of demand.

elimination_hints.hint: Substitute goods (tea and coffee) also follow the law of demand individually. The substitution between goods is actually one reason WHY the law holds — not an exception to it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing substitution between goods with an exception to the law of demand.

question_text: A movement along the demand curve is caused by:
explanation: A movement along the demand curve (also called change in quantity demanded or expansion/contraction of demand) is caused by a change in the PRICE of the good itself, other things remaining constant. When price falls, the consumer moves down along the curve (extension of demand — more quantity demanded). When price rises, the consumer moves up along the curve (contraction of demand — less quantity demanded). Changes in other factors (income, tastes, prices of related goods) cause a SHIFT of the entire demand curve, not movement along it.
subject: economics

options.text: A change in the consumer's income
A change in the consumer's tastes and preferences
A change in the price of a related good
A change in the price of the good itself

elimination_hints.hint: A change in income causes a SHIFT of the demand curve (to the right if income increases for normal goods), not a movement along it. Income is one of the 'other things' held constant.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a shift of the demand curve (income change) with movement along it (price change).

elimination_hints.hint: Changes in tastes and preferences shift the entire demand curve. If a good becomes more fashionable, demand shifts right at every price level. This is a shift, not a movement along.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a demand shift factor (tastes) with the price change that causes movement along the curve.

elimination_hints.hint: A change in the price of a related good (substitute or complement) shifts the demand curve for this good. For example, if the price of tea rises, demand for coffee shifts right. This is a shift, not movement along.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cross-price effects (shift) with own-price effects (movement along).

question_text: The substitution effect of a price decrease means that:
explanation: The substitution effect of a price decrease means the consumer buys more of the good that has become relatively cheaper and less of the other good that is now relatively more expensive. When the price of Good X falls, X becomes cheaper relative to Y (even if Y's price hasn't changed). The consumer substitutes X for Y — buying more X and less Y — to maximise utility. The substitution effect always works in the direction of the law of demand (price falls → buy more). It is isolated by keeping real income constant (Slutsky) or keeping utility constant (Hicks).
subject: economics

options.text: The consumer buys less of the cheaper good because they prefer expensive goods
The consumer substitutes the relatively cheaper good for the relatively more expensive good
The consumer's real income increases and they buy more of all goods
The consumer's tastes change in favour of the cheaper good

elimination_hints.hint: Rational consumers buy MORE of the cheaper good (substitution towards it), not less. Preferring expensive goods is Veblen/snob behaviour — an exception, not the standard substitution effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the direction of the substitution effect.

elimination_hints.hint: This describes the INCOME effect, not the substitution effect. The income effect is about increased purchasing power; the substitution effect is about changed relative prices. They are distinct components of the total price effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the income effect (real income change) with the substitution effect (relative price change).

elimination_hints.hint: The substitution effect does NOT involve a change in tastes. Tastes remain constant — only relative prices change. The consumer switches because the price ratio changed, not because their preferences changed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking the substitution effect involves taste changes rather than price ratio changes.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The demand schedule shows the inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded.
Reasoning (R): A demand schedule is a tabular representation of the law of demand.
explanation: Assertion A is TRUE — the demand schedule is a table listing different prices and the corresponding quantities demanded, showing the inverse relationship (as price rises, Qd falls). Reasoning R is also TRUE — a demand schedule is indeed the tabular form of the law of demand; it presents the same information as the demand curve but in table format. R correctly explains A because the demand schedule (R) is designed to show the law of demand, which is the inverse price-quantity relationship (A).
subject: economics

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A. The demand schedule shows the inverse relationship (A) precisely because it is the tabular representation of the law of demand (R). The law of demand is what generates the inverse pattern in the schedule.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising the direct connection between the law of demand and its tabular representation.

elimination_hints.hint: R is true — a demand schedule is universally recognised as the tabular form of the law of demand, listing price-quantity pairs that demonstrate the inverse relationship.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing what a demand schedule represents.

elimination_hints.hint: A is true — the demand schedule clearly shows that as price goes up, quantity demanded goes down. This is the fundamental information a demand schedule conveys.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the inverse relationship shown in a standard demand schedule.

question_text:
Assertion (A): An increase in consumer income causes a rightward shift of the demand curve for a normal good.
Reasoning (R): When income rises, consumers can afford to buy more of a normal good at every price level.
explanation: Assertion A is TRUE — for normal goods, higher income increases demand at every price, shifting the demand curve to the right. Reasoning R is also TRUE — increased income expands the consumer's budget, allowing them to purchase more. R correctly explains A because the ability to buy more at every price (R) is exactly what causes the rightward shift (A). Note: for inferior goods, the relationship is reversed — higher income decreases demand.
subject: economics

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A. The rightward shift (A) happens specifically because consumers can afford more at every price (R). R is the causal mechanism behind A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not connecting the income increase mechanism with the demand shift.

elimination_hints.hint: R is true — higher income does increase purchasing power, enabling consumers to buy more of normal goods at every given price. This is the standard income effect for normal goods.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying that higher income enables more purchases of normal goods.

elimination_hints.hint: A is true for normal goods — this is the standard textbook result. Higher income shifts demand right for normal goods. (For inferior goods, demand would shift left.)
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distinguishing between normal goods (income increase → demand increase) and inferior goods.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Veblen goods have an upward-sloping demand curve because consumers buy more when prices rise.
Reasoning (R): Veblen goods are status symbols where higher price signals higher prestige and exclusivity.
explanation: Assertion A is TRUE — Veblen goods (luxury status goods like designer handbags, premium watches) do show upward-sloping demand over certain price ranges. When prices rise, their 'status value' increases and affluent consumers buy more to signal wealth. Reasoning R is also TRUE — the Veblen effect occurs because consumers derive utility not just from the good itself but from the prestige associated with its high price. R correctly explains A because the status/prestige motive (R) is the direct cause of the upward-sloping demand (A).
subject: economics

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains A. The upward-sloping demand (A) occurs precisely because of the prestige/status motive (R). If the status motive were absent, demand would follow the normal downward slope.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising that the status motive is the direct cause of Veblen goods' unusual demand behaviour.

elimination_hints.hint: R is true — Veblen goods are well-documented in economics. Conspicuous consumption (Thorstein Veblen, 1899) describes how wealthy consumers buy expensive goods specifically because they are expensive.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the existence of status-driven consumption behaviour.

elimination_hints.hint: A is true — Veblen goods are a recognised exception to the law of demand. Empirical examples include luxury brands where demand increases with price over certain ranges.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all goods must follow the standard downward-sloping demand, ignoring well-documented exceptions.

question_text: The term 'ceteris paribus' in the law of demand means 'other things remaining constant'.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. 'Ceteris paribus' is a Latin phrase meaning 'all other things being equal' or 'other things remaining constant'. In the context of the law of demand, it means that while we study the relationship between price and quantity demanded, all other factors (income, tastes, prices of related goods, expectations, number of buyers) are assumed to remain unchanged. This assumption is crucial because if multiple factors change simultaneously, we cannot isolate the effect of price on demand.
subject: economics

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Ceteris paribus is correctly defined as 'other things remaining constant'. It is the most common assumption in economics, used to isolate the effect of one variable while holding all others fixed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing the meaning of this fundamental economic term.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). Only True or False are valid choices.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A — placeholder option.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). Only True or False are valid choices.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A — placeholder option.

question_text: A shift in the demand curve and a movement along the demand curve are caused by the same factors.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. They are caused by DIFFERENT factors. A movement along the demand curve (expansion or contraction) is caused only by a change in the PRICE of the good itself. A shift of the demand curve (increase or decrease in demand) is caused by changes in OTHER factors: income, tastes, prices of related goods, expectations, population, etc. This is one of the most important distinctions in demand analysis — 'change in quantity demanded' (movement along) vs 'change in demand' (shift of curve).
subject: economics

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: Movement along = own price change. Shift = change in other determinants (income, tastes, related goods' prices). These are fundamentally different causes producing different effects on the demand diagram.
elimination_hints.misconception: Conflating two distinct concepts in demand analysis — movement along and shift of the curve.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). Only True or False are valid choices.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A — placeholder option.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). Only True or False are valid choices.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A — placeholder option.

question_text: For inferior goods, the income effect of a price fall works against the law of demand.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. For inferior goods, the income effect works in the opposite direction to the substitution effect. When price falls: Substitution effect → buy MORE (always supports the law). Income effect → real income rises → for inferior goods, demand FALLS (works against the law). For most inferior goods, the substitution effect is stronger, so the law of demand still holds. But for Giffen goods (a special extreme case of inferior goods), the income effect is so strong that it dominates the substitution effect, causing the law of demand to be violated.
subject: economics

options.text: True
False

elimination_hints.hint: For inferior goods, higher real income (from a price fall) reduces their demand because consumers switch to better alternatives. This negative income effect opposes the positive substitution effect, working against the law of demand.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding how the income effect operates differently for normal vs inferior goods.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). Only True or False are valid choices.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A — placeholder option.

elimination_hints.hint: This option is not applicable (---). Only True or False are valid choices.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A — placeholder option.

question_text: The graphical representation of the law of demand is called the _________ curve, which slopes _________ from left to right.
explanation: The law of demand is graphically represented by the DEMAND curve, which slopes DOWNWARD from left to right. The downward slope visually captures the inverse relationship between price (on the y-axis) and quantity demanded (on the x-axis). As we move right along the x-axis (more quantity), we move down on the y-axis (lower price). This negative slope is the defining characteristic of the demand curve and directly reflects the law of demand.
subject: economics

options.text: Demand; Downward
Supply; Upward
Demand; Upward
Indifference; Downward

elimination_hints.hint: The supply curve slopes upward — showing that producers supply more at higher prices. The demand curve is different: it slopes downward because consumers demand less at higher prices.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the demand curve (downward) with the supply curve (upward).

elimination_hints.hint: The demand curve slopes DOWNWARD, not upward. An upward slope would mean consumers buy more at higher prices, which contradicts the law of demand (except for Giffen/Veblen goods).
elimination_hints.misconception: Getting the direction of the demand curve wrong.

elimination_hints.hint: An indifference curve also slopes downward but represents equal satisfaction, not the price-quantity relationship. The law of demand is represented by the demand curve specifically.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the indifference curve (equal utility) with the demand curve (price-quantity relationship).

question_text: When the price of tea increases, the demand for coffee (a substitute) is likely to _________. This is known as _________ demand.
explanation: When the price of tea increases, consumers switch to coffee (which is now relatively cheaper). This INCREASES the demand for coffee. This relationship between the price of one good and the demand for its substitute is called CROSS demand (or cross-price demand). Cross demand shows how the demand for one good is affected by changes in the price of a related good. For substitutes, cross-price effect is positive (price of one rises → demand for the other rises). For complements, it is negative.
subject: economics

options.text: Decrease; Joint
Increase; Cross
Increase; Derived
Decrease; Cross

elimination_hints.hint: Demand for coffee INCREASES (not decreases) when tea becomes expensive — consumers switch to the cheaper substitute. Joint demand refers to complementary goods, not substitutes.
elimination_hints.misconception: Getting both the direction and the type of demand wrong.

elimination_hints.hint: The direction is correct (increase), but 'derived demand' refers to demand for factors of production (labour, capital) that is derived from demand for final goods. The correct term here is 'cross demand'.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing cross demand (related consumer goods) with derived demand (factors of production).

elimination_hints.hint: The term 'cross demand' is correct, but the direction is wrong. For substitutes, demand INCREASES when the other good's price rises. Consumers switch to the substitute that is now relatively cheaper.
elimination_hints.misconception: Correctly identifying cross demand but getting the direction wrong for substitute goods.

question_text:
Scenario: The market demand schedule for mangoes is as follows:

Price (₹/kg): 100, 80, 60, 40, 20
Quantity demanded (kg): 200, 400, 700, 1100, 1600

Question: What happens to quantity demanded when price falls from ₹80 to ₹60?
explanation: When price falls from ₹80 to ₹60: Quantity demanded increases from 400 kg to 700 kg — an increase of 300 kg. This is a movement ALONG the demand curve (extension/expansion of demand) because it is caused by a change in the price of the good itself. The inverse relationship is clearly visible: lower price → higher quantity demanded. This demonstrates the law of demand. The change represents a 75% increase in quantity (300/400 × 100) for a 25% decrease in price (20/80 × 100).
subject: economics

options.text: Quantity demanded falls from 400 to 200 kg
Quantity demanded remains at 400 kg
Quantity demanded increases from 400 to 700 kg (extension of demand)
The demand curve shifts rightward

elimination_hints.hint: 200 kg corresponds to a price of ₹100, not ₹60. At ₹60, the schedule clearly shows 700 kg. A price FALL should INCREASE quantity demanded, not decrease it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reading the wrong row of the demand schedule and contradicting the law of demand.

elimination_hints.hint: 400 kg is the quantity at ₹80, the starting price. At ₹60, the new quantity is 700 kg. The law of demand ensures that a price fall changes the quantity demanded — it doesn't remain constant.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking quantity demanded stays fixed when price changes.

elimination_hints.hint: A shift of the demand curve requires a change in factors OTHER than price (income, tastes, etc.). Here, only price changed, so we have a MOVEMENT ALONG the existing curve, not a shift.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing movement along the demand curve (price change) with a shift of the curve (other factors change).

question_text:
Scenario: During a severe flood, the price of bottled water rises from ₹20 to ₹100 per bottle. Despite the price increase, people buy even more bottled water than before because tap water is contaminated.

Question: Does this situation violate the law of demand?
explanation: This does NOT violate the law of demand. The law of demand requires 'other things remaining constant' (ceteris paribus). In this scenario, other things have NOT remained constant — the availability of tap water (a substitute) has changed dramatically due to contamination. This is equivalent to a SHIFT in the demand curve for bottled water (demand increases due to changed circumstances), not a movement along it. The ceteris paribus condition is violated because the quality/availability of substitutes changed simultaneously with the price change. For a true violation, the price increase alone (with no other changes) would need to increase quantity demanded.
subject: economics

options.text: Yes, it violates the law because people buy more at a higher price
No, the ceteris paribus condition is not met — demand shifted rightward due to contamination of tap water (a substitute)
Yes, bottled water has become a Giffen good
Yes, this proves the law of demand is always wrong

elimination_hints.hint: Simply observing higher price and higher quantity does not violate the law. The law requires ceteris paribus. Here, other things changed (tap water became unsafe), shifting the entire demand curve rightward. Both price and demand can increase if the curve shifts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the ceteris paribus condition and incorrectly concluding a law violation.

elimination_hints.hint: Bottled water has not become a Giffen good. A Giffen good has an upward-sloping demand curve due to a dominant income effect. Here, the demand curve itself has shifted right due to changed circumstances — a completely different phenomenon.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a demand shift (changed conditions) with a Giffen good (inherent upward-sloping demand).

elimination_hints.hint: One scenario with confounding factors does not disprove the law. The law holds under ceteris paribus conditions. This scenario violates those conditions, so it is not a valid test of the law.
elimination_hints.misconception: Making sweeping generalisations from a single case that doesn't even meet the law's stated conditions.

question_text:
Match the following determinants of demand (Column I) with their effects on the demand curve for a normal good (Column II):

Column I:
P. Increase in consumer income
Q. Fall in the price of a substitute good
R. Rise in the price of the good itself
S. Favourable change in consumer tastes

Column II:
1. Demand curve shifts rightward
2. Demand curve shifts leftward
3. Movement upward along the demand curve (contraction)
4. No change in the demand curve
explanation: P → 1: Higher income for a normal good shifts demand right (consumers buy more at every price). Q → 2: Fall in substitute's price means consumers switch TO the substitute and AWAY from this good — demand shifts left. R → 3: Rise in own price causes movement UP along the demand curve (contraction) — less quantity demanded but the curve itself doesn't shift. S → 1: Favourable taste change (the good becomes more popular) shifts demand right — consumers want more at every price.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: A maps Q (fall in substitute's price) to rightward shift (1). This is wrong — if a substitute becomes cheaper, consumers switch to it, REDUCING demand for this good (leftward shift, 2).
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding that a cheaper substitute draws consumers away, reducing demand for this good.

elimination_hints.hint: B maps P (income increase) to leftward shift (2). For a NORMAL good, income increase shifts demand RIGHT (1). A leftward shift for income increase would apply to an INFERIOR good, not a normal good.
elimination_hints.misconception: Applying the inferior good response to a normal good.

elimination_hints.hint: D maps S (favourable taste change) to leftward shift (2). If tastes shift in FAVOUR of a good, demand INCREASES (rightward shift, 1). A leftward shift would occur if tastes turned AGAINST the good.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the effect of favourable taste changes on demand.

question_text:
Match the following exceptions to the law of demand (Column I) with their correct description (Column II):

Column I:
P. Giffen goods
Q. Veblen goods
R. Speculation-driven demand
S. Necessities during emergencies

Column II:
1. Demand rises with price because higher price signals prestige and status
2. Consumers expect further price rises and buy more now at higher prices
3. Inferior goods where the income effect of a price rise dominates the substitution effect
4. Demand remains high regardless of price because the good is essential for survival
explanation: P → 3: Giffen goods are inferior goods where the income effect (price rise reduces real income, forcing more consumption of the cheap staple) dominates the substitution effect. Q → 1: Veblen goods are luxury/prestige goods where higher price increases desirability because it signals status and exclusivity. R → 2: When consumers expect prices to rise further, they buy more now even at current higher prices (speculative demand). S → 4: During emergencies (floods, famines), essentials like food and medicine are bought regardless of price because survival is at stake.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: B swaps P and Q. Giffen goods (P) are inferior goods with dominant income effect (3), not prestige goods (1). Veblen goods (Q) are prestige/status goods (1), not inferior goods (3).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Giffen goods (inferior, poor consumers) with Veblen goods (luxury, wealthy consumers).

elimination_hints.hint: C swaps Q and R. Veblen goods (Q) are about prestige (1), not speculation (2). Speculation (R) is about expected future price rises (2), not prestige (1).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing status-driven demand (Veblen) with expectation-driven demand (speculation).

elimination_hints.hint: D swaps R and S. Speculation (R) is about buying more now expecting higher future prices (2). Emergencies (S) are about essential survival needs (4), not price expectations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing speculative demand (future expectations) with emergency demand (survival needs).

question_text:
Arrange the following events in the correct causal sequence when the price of a normal good falls:

1. Consumer buys more of the good (quantity demanded increases)
2. The good becomes relatively cheaper compared to substitutes
3. Price of the good falls
4. Consumer's real income increases (purchasing power rises)
explanation: The correct sequence is: 3 → 2 → 4 → 1. First, the price falls (3) — this is the triggering event. Then, two simultaneous effects occur: the good becomes relatively cheaper (substitution effect, 2) and the consumer's real income rises (income effect, 4). Finally, as a result of both effects, the consumer buys more of the good (1). While 2 and 4 happen simultaneously in reality, logically the substitution effect (relative price change) is the more immediate and direct effect.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: A starts with the consumer buying more (1) before the price even falls (3). The effect cannot precede the cause. Price fall must come first.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing the result (more buying) before the cause (price fall).

elimination_hints.hint: B places the consumer buying more (1) before the substitution (2) and income (4) effects. But the consumer buys more BECAUSE of these effects. The effects must be recognised before the behavioural response.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing the behavioural outcome before the underlying economic effects that cause it.

elimination_hints.hint: C places the income effect (4) before the substitution effect (2). While both occur simultaneously, the substitution effect (relative price change) is the more immediate and direct effect of a price change.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ordering the substitution and income effects incorrectly in the causal chain.

question_text:
Arrange the following types of demand in order from the most basic/direct to the most derived/indirect:

1. Derived demand (demand for factors of production)
2. Individual demand (one consumer's demand)
3. Market demand (sum of all individual demands)
4. Composite demand (demand for a good with multiple uses)
explanation: The correct order from most basic to most derived is: 2 → 3 → 4 → 1. Individual demand (2) is the most basic — a single consumer's price-quantity relationship. Market demand (3) is the horizontal summation of all individual demands. Composite demand (4) is more complex — demand for a good that serves multiple purposes (like steel for cars, buildings, and machinery). Derived demand (1) is the most indirect — demand for inputs (labour, raw materials) that is derived from the demand for the final product they help produce.
subject: economics

elimination_hints.hint: A starts with derived demand (1), which is the MOST indirect type. The sequence should start with the most basic (individual demand) and progress to the most derived.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the order — starting with the most complex instead of the most basic.

elimination_hints.hint: B places market demand (3) before individual demand (2). But market demand is built FROM individual demands (horizontal summation). The individual level is more basic than the aggregate market level.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking market-level demand is more basic than individual-level demand.

elimination_hints.hint: D places composite demand (4) before market demand (3). Market demand (aggregation of individuals) is a more straightforward concept than composite demand (one good with multiple uses), making it the logical next step after individual.
elimination_hints.misconception: Placing a more complex demand concept before a simpler aggregation concept.

question_text:
Consider a demand curve diagram where Price is on the y-axis and Quantity Demanded is on the x-axis. Initially, the demand curve is D₁. Due to a significant increase in consumer income, the demand curve shifts to D₂ (to the right of D₁). At the same time, the price of the good increases from P₁ to P₂.

Question: What is the net effect on quantity demanded?
explanation: Two things happen simultaneously: (1) Income increase shifts demand right (D₁ → D₂), increasing quantity demanded at every price. (2) Price increase from P₁ to P₂ causes movement upward along D₂, reducing quantity demanded. The net effect depends on the relative magnitudes. If the rightward shift is large relative to the price increase, the net quantity demanded increases. If the price increase is large relative to the demand shift, the net quantity could decrease. Without specific numbers, the net effect is ambiguous — it depends on which effect dominates. Both effects are at work simultaneously.
subject: economics

options.text: Quantity demanded definitely increases because income increased
Quantity demanded definitely decreases because price increased
Quantity demanded remains the same because the two effects cancel out
The net effect is indeterminate — it depends on whether the demand shift or the price change is larger

elimination_hints.hint: While the income increase does shift demand right, the simultaneous price increase works in the opposite direction (reducing quantity along D₂). Without knowing magnitudes, we cannot conclude quantity definitely increases.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the price increase effect and considering only the income effect.

elimination_hints.hint: While the price increase reduces quantity along the new curve, the demand shift itself has increased the quantity at every price. The two opposing effects mean the net outcome is uncertain without specific data.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the demand shift and considering only the price change.

elimination_hints.hint: The two effects do not necessarily cancel out. Exact cancellation would be a coincidence. The net effect depends on relative magnitudes — one effect could easily dominate the other.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming opposing effects must perfectly cancel, which is a special case, not the general outcome.
